resume:
Senior Supply Chain Manager | End-to-End Planning | S&OP | Demand-Driven MRP | Lean | Six Sigma

Results-oriented supply chain professional with deep cross-functional expertise spanning demand planning, supply planning, inventory optimization, procurement, logistics, and S&OP orchestration. Proven track record leveraging data-driven methodologies to drive best-in-class service levels and working-capital efficiency.

Core competencies: Demand Forecasting | Statistical Modeling | Safety Stock Optimization | MEIO | DDMRP | Sales & Operations Planning | Sales & Operations Execution | Supplier Relationship Management | Logistics & Distribution | Network Optimization | SAP IBP | Kinaxis RapidResponse | o9 | Power BI | SQL | Lean Manufacturing | Six Sigma Green Belt | Continuous Improvement | KPI Dashboards | Cross-Functional Leadership

Experience:
Senior Supply Chain Manager, consumer electronics (2019-present) — Own end-to-end planning for a $400M product portfolio; lead monthly S&OP; drive inventory and service-level programs.
Supply Chain Manager, CPG (2015-2019) — Demand & supply planning, supplier scorecards, distribution optimization.
Demand Planner, industrial goods (2012-2015).

Education: B.S. Supply Chain Management. APICS CPIM, CSCP certified.
jd: We are seeking a Senior Supply Chain Manager to own end-to-end planning for a fast-scaling consumer hardware business, reporting to the Director of Supply Chain. You will run S&OP, own forecast accuracy and inventory health, and balance service level against working capital across a global supplier base with long lead times. The mandate: lift forecast accuracy (currently MAPE ~38% at the SKU-week level), reduce excess-and-obsolete inventory, and protect a 97% line-fill target through demand volatility and component constraints. We want a planner who can be specific about methodology — how they segment SKUs, set safety stock, run the S&OP consensus, and make the service-vs-cash tradeoff. You will defend planning decisions to commercial and finance leadership.

history.q: Describe your current end-to-end planning scope — portfolio size, what you own, who you partner with.
What's your current SKU-level forecast accuracy, and how do you measure it — MAPE, bias, at what aggregation?
Walk me through one change you made that actually improved forecast accuracy.
How do you segment your SKUs for planning — and what do you do differently across segments?
How do you set safety stock today? Give me the actual method, not the tool name.
Tell me about a stockout that hit service level. What was the root cause and what did you change?
Describe your monthly S&OP cycle — the steps and who owns each one.
Give me a specific S&OP meeting where demand and supply disagreed. How was it resolved, and by whom?
How do you handle the tradeoff between line-fill and excess inventory when a component is constrained?
Tell me about an excess-and-obsolete write-down you were close to. What drove it?
When forecast and the sales team's number diverge, whose number goes into the plan and why?
Walk me through a supplier capacity constraint you had to plan around.
What single planning KPI do you watch most closely day to day?
Our SKU-week MAPE is around 38%. Where would you start — segmentation, the statistical model, or the consensus process?

candidate_last_answer: Before I tell you where I'd start, I'd actually want to turn it around and ask you a few things — because 38% MAPE at SKU-week could mean very different problems. First: what's your MAPE at the SKU-month and category level? If it's fine at category but blows up at SKU-week, that's a mix/timing problem, not a model problem. Second: how much of the portfolio is new products with no history versus mature SKUs? And third: is the 38% volume-weighted or simple-average across SKUs, because a few low-volume long-tail SKUs can wreck a simple average. Honestly, wouldn't you agree it's premature for me to say 'fix the model' before we know which of those it is? So — what do those cuts actually look like on your side?

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: wouldn't you agree it's premature for me to say 'fix the model'

expected-yield: grant him every clarifying answer (38% is volume-weighted at SKU-week, ~25% of portfolio is new products, category-level MAPE is healthy) and then force him to actually commit to a diagnostic sequence and the first concrete intervention he would make, exposing whether he has a real methodology behind the clarifying questions
